dating.basis: Bhāskara states his own birth year (Śaka 1036) and composition date in the Golādhyāya; a 1207 inscription records an endowment for the study of his works.

summary: The high point of classical Indian mathematics: the Līlāvatī (arithmetic, addressed to a woman — traditionally his daughter), Bījagaṇita (algebra: negatives, surds, the cakravāla cyclic method solving Pell-type equations like x² = 61y² + 1, five centuries before Fermat posed it), and Siddhāntaśiromaṇi (astronomy, with instantaneous velocity of planets computed via a sine-difference relation equivalent to a derivative).
whyItMatters: The cakravāla and the instantaneous-motion work are the standard exhibits of pre-modern Indian mathematics at the research frontier of its age — recognized as such in mainstream history of mathematics, no inflation required.
honestyNote: 'Bhāskara invented calculus' overstates it: he computed an instantaneous rate in one astronomical context; there is no general theory of limits or integration here — that synthesis belongs to the Kerala school (for series) and to Newton/Leibniz (for the full calculus). The Līlāvatī 'grieving daughter' frame story is late legend, not documented biography.

locations.name: Vijjadavida (identified near Chalisgaon, Maharashtra); worked at Ujjain observatory tradition

sources.label: Colebrooke, H.T. (tr.), Algebra, with Arithmetic and Mensuration, from the Sanscrit of Brahmegupta and Bháscara (1817)
Plofker, K., Mathematics in India (2009), ch. 6 — cakravāla and instantaneous motion
Patwardhan, K.S. et al., Līlāvatī of Bhāskarācārya (Motilal Banarsidass, 2001)

image.alt: Manuscript folio of the Līlāvatī tradition (Wellcome Hindi MS 803)

dharmicTie.statement: The capstone of siddhānta mathematics, composed inside the tradition's scholarly culture — the Līlāvatī opens with an invocation to Gaṇeśa, and a 1207 inscription endows a maṭha specifically to study his works.

scriptureRefs.text: the Gaṇeśa invocation opening the arithmetic

dating.display: c. 700–750 CE (mainstream); monastery traditions range from 509 BCE to 788–820 CE
dating.basis: No contemporary records; dated by citation networks (he quotes Dharmakīrti; is answered by later authors) and doxographic position.

positions.evidence: He cites 7th-century Buddhist logicians; his opponents' and successors' dates bracket him.

positions.holders: Widely repeated conventional date; some maṭha records
positions.evidence: Late hagiographies; convenient but weakly grounded.
Monastic succession lists; incompatible with the citation evidence; reported as tradition.

summary: Philosopher-monk from Kaladi (Kerala) who systematized Advaita Vedānta — nondual brahman, the world as neither simply real nor unreal, liberation through knowledge — in commentaries on the Upaniṣads, Gītā, and Brahmasūtras that set the format of Indian philosophical debate for a millennium. Tradition credits him with founding four (or more) monastic seats and reorganizing ascetic orders.
whyItMatters: Arguably the most influential Hindu philosopher, and simultaneously the atlas's best case study in dating honesty: a figure whose ideas are precisely documented and whose century is genuinely uncertain — with the tradition's own institutions disagreeing by 1,200 years.
honestyNote: Hundreds of works circulate under his name; text-critics accept a much smaller core (the major commentaries and Upadeśasāhasrī). The 'digvijaya' biographies are centuries-later hagiography.

locations.name: Kaladi, Ernakulam District, Kerala, India — traditional birthplace

sources.label: Potter, K. (ed.), Encyclopedia of Indian Philosophies, Vol. III: Advaita Vedānta up to Śaṃkara (1981)
Isayeva, N., Shankara and Indian Philosophy (SUNY, 1993)
Harimoto, K., "The Date of Śaṅkara: Between the Cālukyas and the Rāṣṭrakūṭas", Journal of Indological Studies (2006)

dharmicTie.statement: His work IS scripture-commentary: the Advaita he systematized is argued verse by verse on the prasthānatrayī, and the monastic orders attributed to him remain living institutions of the tradition.

scriptureRefs.text: athāto brahmajijñāsā — 'now, therefore, the inquiry into Brahman'

dating.display: substantially 8th century CE (Rāṣṭrakūṭa Kṛṣṇa I, r. c. 756–773), with later additions
dating.basis: Epigraphic and stylistic evidence; a copper-plate grant of Karka II (812 CE) already describes the temple as astonishing its own architects.

summary: A full-scale Śiva temple — courtyard, gateway, pavilion, shrine tower, life-size elephants — carved DOWNWARD out of a single basalt hillside, removing an estimated 200,000+ tonnes of rock. Not built: excavated, as one piece, to imitate Mount Kailāsa.
whyItMatters: The most audacious rock-cut project of the ancient world and the summit of a thousand-year Indian cave-architecture tradition (Barabar → Ajanta → Elephanta → Ellora). Its 'impossible' reputation feeds ancient-aliens-style content — the honest wonder (planning, logistics, generations of masons) needs no aliens.
honestyNote: Construction sequence and exact duration are debated (single reign vs multiple phases); 'built in 18 years by unknown technology' claims are content-farm inventions. Chisels, measured planning, and dynastic resources are the documented technology.

locations.name: Ellora Caves (cave 16), Aurangabad District, Maharashtra, India

sources.label: UNESCO World Heritage Centre: Ellora Caves (1983)
Dhavalikar, M.K., Ellora (Oxford Monuments series, 2003)
Goetz, H., "The Kailasa of Ellora and the Chronology of Rashtrakuta Art", Artibus Asiae 15 (1952)
Archaeological Survey of India — Ellora records

dharmicTie.statement: A Śiva temple carved as Mount Kailāsa itself — the purāṇic abode of Śiva rendered in stone. Iconographic program (Rāvaṇa shaking Kailāsa, Śaiva and Vaiṣṇava reliefs) is scriptural narrative throughout.

scriptureRefs.ref: Śiva Purāṇa (Kailāsa as Śiva's abode); Rāmāyaṇa episodes in the reliefs
scriptureRefs.text: the temple's program is purāṇic narrative in stone

custody.holder: Archaeological Survey of India; UNESCO World Heritage (1983)
custody.place: Ellora, Chhatrapati Sambhajinagar (Aurangabad) district, Maharashtra, India
custody.legalBasis: AMASR Act, 1958; World Heritage Convention (1983 inscription). Being living rock, it is the rare masterpiece that cannot be trafficked — only neglected.

parallels.name: Great Temple of Ramesses II, Abu Simbel
parallels.note: the world's other great rock-cut colossus

parallels.name: Longmen Grottoes
parallels.note: rock-cut sacred complex; heavily looted in the 20th c. — the counter-example custody law exists for

dating.display: iconography matured c. 10th century CE (Chola dynasty); casting tradition continuous to the present
dating.basis: Dated temple commissions and inscriptions; the canonical form (dance of bliss within the flaming circle) is fixed under the imperial Cholas.

summary: Śiva as Lord of the Dance: drum of creation, fire of dissolution, raised foot of release, dwarf of ignorance underfoot — theology compressed into a bronze form cast by the lost-wax process in the same craft lineage attested at Mohenjo-daro 3,500 years earlier, and still cast today by hereditary sthapatis in Swamimalai.
whyItMatters: Among the most recognized religious artworks on Earth and a favorite of modern physics culture (Coomaraswamy's 1918 essay 'The Dance of Śiva'; the 2m Naṭarāja gifted by India to CERN in 2004). Also central to modern repatriation law: stolen Chola bronzes have driven landmark art-trafficking cases and returns.
honestyNote: 'The Naṭarāja encodes modern cosmology' is metaphor, not physics — CERN's statue carries a plaque quoting Fritjof Capra's analogy, and an analogy is what it is. The craft continuity (lost-wax) is documented; identity of religious meaning across 3,500 years is not claimed.

locations.name: Chidambaram Naṭarāja temple, Tamil Nadu — the cult's center
Swamimalai, Thanjavur District — living casting tradition

sources.label: Coomaraswamy, A.K., "The Dance of Śiva" (1918)
Dehejia, V., The Sensuous and the Sacred: Chola Bronzes from South India (2002)
Srinivasan, S., "Shiva as 'cosmic dancer': on Pallava origins for the Nataraja bronze", World Archaeology 36 (2004) — archaeometallurgy and dating

dharmicTie.statement: Scripturally constituted and continuously worshipped: the ānanda-tāṇḍava form is expounded in the Tamil Śaiva canon, consecrated bronzes are processional deities in active temple worship (which is why their theft is sacrilege, not just larceny — see custody), and the casting lineage is unbroken.

scriptureRefs.text: the dance of Śiva as cosmic act — the theology the icon compresses
the classical exposition of the Naṭarāja symbolism (drum, fire, foot of release)

custody.holder: Living temples (administered under Tamil Nadu's HR&CE Act, 1959) and museums worldwide
custody.place: Chidambaram and temple Tamil Nadu (in worship); Government Museum Chennai; and diaspora holdings from New York to Canberra
custody.legalBasis: In India: temple property under HR&CE administration; export barred by the Antiquities and Art Treasures Act, 1972. Abroad: acquisitions contested case by case — the London Bumper judgment (1991) returned the Pathur Naṭarāja on the doctrine that the consecrated deity is a juristic person; UNESCO 1970 governs post-1970 trafficking.

parallels.name: Śiva Naṭarāja (c. 950 CE)
parallels.holder: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York

parallels.name: Naṭarāja (the Pathur bronze)
parallels.holder: returned to Tamil Nadu, 1991, via UK courts

parallels.name: Dancing Śiva
parallels.holder: National Gallery of Australia — returned to India 2014 after the Kapoor exposures

dating.display: Tamil Āḻvārs and Nāyaṉmār c. 6th–9th century CE; successive regional waves through the 17th century
dating.basis: The saint-poets' corpora and hagiographies are datable regionally; 'movement' singular is a modern historiographic frame (Hawley) for linked regional surges.

summary: Devotion as direct access: Tamil poet-saints singing to Viṣṇu and Śiva in the vernacular, then wave after regional wave — Vīraśaivas in Karnataka (Basava, Akka Mahādēvī), Varkaris in Maharashtra (Jñāneśvar, Tukārām, Nāmdev), Mīrābāī in Rajasthan, Caitanya in Bengal, Ravidās and Kabīr in the north — poetry over priestcraft, often explicitly against caste exclusion, composed by weavers, cobblers, women.
whyItMatters: Bhakti reshaped Hinduism into the devotional religion most Hindus actually practice, created the classical literatures of a dozen modern languages, and preserves the tradition's own internal critique of hierarchy — indispensable context for ledger entries on caste and reform.
honestyNote: Romantic overreading is easy in both directions: bhakti saints were not modern social revolutionaries, and yet the anti-caste voice in their poetry is textually real (Ravidās, Kabīr, the Vīraśaiva vacanas). 'One unified movement' is a modern narrative convenience — Hawley's study traces how that framing was built.

sources.label: Hawley, J.S., A Storm of Songs: India and the Idea of the Bhakti Movement (Harvard, 2015)
Ramanujan, A.K., Speaking of Śiva (Penguin, 1973) — vacana translations
Novetzke, C., Religion and Public Memory: A Cultural History of Saint Namdev in India (Columbia, 2008)

dharmicTie.statement: The devotional mainstream of the tradition as practiced today, with its charter in smṛti itself.

scriptureRefs.text: patraṁ puṣpaṁ phalaṁ toyam — the leaf-flower-fruit-water verse of accessible devotion

dating.display: founded 5th century CE (Gupta, Kumāragupta I); functioned ~700 years; destroyed/abandoned c. 1200 CE
dating.basis: Seals and inscriptions from the site fix the Gupta foundation; Xuanzang (7th c.) and Yijing describe it in operation; Persian chronicle and archaeology mark the end.

summary: THE institutional university of ancient India — endowed by kings, residential, corporate, with admission examinations, a famed library (Dharmagañja), and an international student body from China, Korea, and Southeast Asia. Curriculum: Buddhist philosophy foremost, plus Vedas, logic, grammar, medicine. Xuanzang studied and taught here and carried its texts to China.
whyItMatters: When posts inflate Taxila into 'the world's first university', Nālandā is the casualty: it is the site that actually earns the institutional title. Its destruction around 1200 (Bakhtiyār Khaljī's raids, per the Tabaqāt-i-Nāṣirī) and the end of Buddhist scholasticism in India is among the pivotal ruptures in South Asian intellectual history.
honestyNote: The Tabaqāt-i-Nāṣirī describes the sack of a fortified monastery generally identified with the Nālandā region; the popular details ('library burned for three/six months') are later embellishment. Decline was also underway before the raids. Precision here, as ever, strengthens rather than weakens the historical case.

locations.name: Nalanda, Bihar, India — UNESCO World Heritage (2016)

sources.label: Beal, S. (tr.), Si-yu-ki: Buddhist Records of the Western World (Xuanzang) (1884)
Scharfe, H., Education in Ancient India (2002) — the institutional contrast with Taxila
UNESCO World Heritage Centre: Archaeological Site of Nalanda Mahavihara (2016)
Minhāj-i-Sirāj, Tabaqāt-i-Nāṣirī (tr. Raverty, 1881) — the destruction account
Archaeological Survey of India — Nalanda excavation and site records

dharmicTie.statement: Primarily a Buddhist mahāvihāra — stated honestly — inside the shared Indic knowledge world: Xuanzang records Vedas, grammar, logic, and medicine in its curriculum, and its scholastic method shaped every darśana's debate culture, including Vedānta's.

custody.holder: Archaeological Survey of India; UNESCO World Heritage (2016)

parallels.name: Vikramaśīlā mahāvihāra
parallels.note: sister university, destroyed in the same period

parallels.name: Somapura Mahāvihāra (Paharpur)
parallels.holder: Bangladesh Department of Archaeology — UNESCO listed
parallels.note: the Pāla monastic university in Bengal

parallels.name: Nālandā manuscripts and bronzes
parallels.holder: dispersed: Tibetan monasteries (carried by fleeing monks), museums incl. Nalanda ASI museum
